summary: High-quality product ideal that fully specifies dashboard browser-session auth from bootstrap redirect through API/WebSocket transport to gateway validation and error responses. Entity namespaces (Access, Browser, Gateway, Backend, Policy) create clean topology. Enforces and defines blocks are thorough and precise. Causal flows cover the complete lifecycle including both happy paths and explicit error shapes. Minor density loss from structurally parallel HTTP/WS error chains and one stray comment line. No live-use evidence available from evaluation context alone.
strengths: Complete auth lifecycle from lambda redirect through bootstrap exchange, token storage, API/WS transport, gateway validation, token stripping, and error responses — nothing is left to inference
Entity namespace grouping (Access/Browser/Gateway/Backend/Policy) makes the system topology immediately scannable and recoverable without external context
Defines block precisely eliminates interpretive slack on every key term including subtle distinctions like BootstrapUrlOnly vs NoPostBootstrapTokenInUrl

Abstraction discipline is clean — stays at product-ideal level without leaking implementation details while remaining specific enough to be actionable (e.g. Sec-WebSocket-Protocol as browser-compatible transport)
Error specification is unusually complete: names the missing header, prescribes the status code, and specifies instructional body content for both HTTP and WebSocket channels
weaknesses: HTTP missing-auth and WebSocket missing-auth when/then chains are structurally parallel — could potentially be factored into a shared pattern to reduce token cost without losing clarity
Line 3 comment '// Dashboard Session Auth' violates Agentish's rejection of comments; the role string in the define.system call already carries this information
No live-use evidence is recoverable from the file content or provided companion context — the file references specific systems but nothing confirms active pairing with implementation artifacts
